story:
Arthur Bennet, a handsome retiree, arrives early at his men’s club banquet. The room is elegant—the long head table flanked by two round tables with matching flowers—and he is relieved the affair is semiformal. In one pocket he carries his acceptance speech for club president; in the other, the proposal he plans to make to Trisha Travis, plus the ring box. He settles Trisha at the head table, then sees his daughter Christina arrive with her husband, Robert, and Arthur Bennet’s spry ninety-year-old father. Arthur kisses Trisha’s cheek, hurries to greet his family with warmth, and seats them at the table opposite the head table. Trisha is surprised to find her cousin Louis and his wife seated beside her, but she assumes Arthur will return quickly.

Arthur circulates, wine glass in hand, charming his guests and dropping by Trisha’s chair to murmur endearments. His secretary, Mable, and her husband are the last to arrive; she alone knows both speeches are in Arthur’s pockets. The dinner is elegant, the band plays softly, and Robert—Christina’s husband, a club member and vintner—greets friends with easy humor.

After dessert, a drum roll sounds. The outgoing president announces, “Mr. Arthur Bennet, our new President Elect.” Arthur rises, conscious he’s had too much to drink and too little to eat. He delivers his acceptance with grace, then adds, “As you all know, my late wife has been gone a long time.” Emotion swamps him; black spots gather at the edge of his vision. His father reads it instantly, catches him cleanly, and eases him to the floor.

“Please excuse him,” the elder Bennet says into the mic, eyeing his recovering son. “Emotion fells him. He never eats when he’s excited. On pourrait penser qu’il apprendrait.” The doctor waves everyone back. The mic pops alive again. “Mable, I didn’t know you cared. Are you the one he’s going to declare undying love for? Do you have the divorce papers?” Mable glares. “Arthur warned me about you.” Her husband waves a handful of papers. “I won’t give you a divorce!” They break into laughter, fall into each other’s arms, and kiss to general amusement.

Old Mr. Bennet roams like a stand-up, winning laughs. At Christina and Robert’s table he softens: “These young people are precious to me.” He praises Robert—the Earl of Carswell—for following his heart into wine and the land, a pointed jab at Arthur’s legal career.

He turns back toward the head table just as Trisha starts for Arthur. “Where do you think you’re going, young lady?” He stops her long enough to spot a small velvet box on the floor. “Would you get that box?” Trisha retrieves it; he opens it. “Wow.” Arthur pats his pockets—empty. “Donne-moi cette boîte ou je te ferai arrêter pour grand vol,” he warns, half-playing, half-serious. “Mon fils, je suis content que tu te sentes mieux,” his father replies, handing it over and holding the mic up. “Dis quelque chose.”

Still shaky, Arthur slips into his late mother’s French, then blurts in clear English, “Trisha, will you marry me?” She opens the box and gasps. “It’s so big and brilliant.” She throws her arms around his neck. “Of course.” “A yes will do,” he teases. “Yes,” she breathes, kissing his neck. The mic catches every whisper. Laughter ripples, spoons pound, and the room chants, “Kiss her!” He does. The band slides into “Misty.” He slides the ring onto her finger, draws her close, and they dance like teenagers. Some say she’s holding him up.

When they sit, his father is gone, leaving a note: “Caught cab home. Need sleep for early rise. J. P. Bennet.” Trisha nods; she gave him a front door key. “Christina has the full set,” she adds. Arthur shows Trisha the proposal speech he planned. “That is so beautiful,” she says. They toast with his father’s wine, friends circling to congratulate them, faces blurring as the music carries them through a few more dances.

“Shall we beat the crowd home?” Arthur murmurs. “Your father is there.” He laughs. “The house is big, and our door has a lock.” Then, wryly: “The maître d’ has my keys—doctor’s orders.” They share a cab anyway, and the driver pretends not to notice the mature, newly engaged couple necking all the way home.
